name: OSS Rebuild
summary: Google's ecosystem-scale rebuild verification project (announced 2025) that rebuilds published packages and compares with the upstream artifact; addresses the question 'should this artifact have been trusted in the first place?' against existing ecosystems (PyPI, npm, crates.io) without requiring authors to adopt hermetic build tooling.

sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Google's **OSS Rebuild** (announced 2025) is a newer point in the packaging design space: instead of asking every package author to adopt hermetic build tooling from day one, it attempts to **rebuild published packages at ecosystem scale** and compare the result with the upstream artifact. The goal is not merely reproducibility for one project, but **continuous, third-party validation of public package ecosystems**.

sections.title: Trust model — what registries currently rely on
Distinct from Nix/Guix and Sigstore
sections.content:
The design point is distinct from Nix/Guix (`data/nix-flakes`, `data/guix`). Nix and Guix make reproducibility a property of a hermetic package-definition language and a content-addressed store. OSS Rebuild instead works against **existing ecosystems** such as PyPI, npm, and crates.io with much lower ecosystem-wide migration cost: infer how a package was likely built, rebuild it, and compare outputs. This is weaker than fully hermetic package management, but far easier to deploy across large pre-existing ecosystems.

The complement to Sigstore + SLSA (`data/sigstore-slsa`): instead of cryptographically attesting the build process at publish time, OSS Rebuild attempts to *re-derive* the artifact from the public source after the fact. The two compose: Sigstore proves who built it, SLSA describes how, and OSS Rebuild independently confirms the bytes match.

sections.title: Design lesson — registry-level rebuild verification
sections.content:
The broader design lesson is that **reproducibility can become a registry- or ecosystem-level validation service**, not just a local developer or CI concern. A new language ecosystem could choose to make this kind of rebuild verification a first-class registry feature from the start, rather than retrofitting it after supply-chain incidents.

The key insight: rebuild verification can be deployed unilaterally by a single party (Google, the registry, or a third-party security service) without requiring every package author to change their workflow. This makes it far cheaper to deploy than "every package must be a Nix derivation" or "every author must adopt SLSA Level 3."

sources.label: Google Security Blog — Introducing OSS Rebuild
